1 Defendants the Trustees of the University of San Francisco, the University of San
2 || Francisco, BJ Johnson, Dena Davis, and Nicole Bohn (collectively, “Answering Defendants”),
3 || answer the unverified Third Amended Complaint (“Complaint”) of Plaintiff Patricia Westerfield
4 || (“Plaintiff”), filed on November 16, 2004, as follows:
5 GENERAL DENIAL
6 Pursuant to the provisions of California Code of Civil Procedure §431.30(d), Answering
7 ||Defendants now file a general denial to said Complaint. In answering each and all of the
8 |lallegations of said Complaint, Answering Defendants deny generally and specifically each and
9 ||every allegation. Answering Defendants further deny, generally and specifically, that Plaintiff
10 |\has been damaged in any sum, or at all, by reason of any act or omission on the part of
11 || Answering Defendants, and/or each of them, and/or its or their employees, agents, or any of
12 || them.
13 AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSES
14 FIRST AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
15 1. Answering Defendants assert and allege that the Complaint, and each cause of
16 |\action contained therein, is barred in that Plaintiff failed to exhaust her statutory administrative
17 || remedies which are a prerequisite to Plaintiff's claims.
18 SECOND AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
19 2. As a second affirmative defense, the Complaint fails to state facts sufficient to
20 ||constitute a cause of action against Answering Defendants, including but not limited to the
21 || Ninth and Tenth Causes of Action.
22 THIRD AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
23 3. As a third affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred by the statutes of
24 || limitations, including but not limited to, those set forth in California Code of Civil Procedure
25 || § 340, California Government Code § 12940, et seg., federal ADA, and/or federal ADEA.
26 FOURTILAFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
27 4. As a fourth affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred in that Plaintiff failed to
28 ||exhaust her administrative remedies under employer grievance procedures under the alleged

AMENDED COMPLAINT

1 || contract (personnel manual) and/or failed to petition the court for mandamus.
2 FIFTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
3 5. As a sixth affirmative defense to the Complaint, Plaintiff's claims are barred
4 |land/or damages therefore are limited by the after-acquired-evidence doctrine, based on
5 || Plaintiff's misconduct related to theft, conversion and/or unauthorized removal of confidential
6 || materials from the University, and/or other unauthorized access to University records, and/or
7 || theft of University time while performing these acts of misconduct while drawing salary from
8 |Ithe University as an employee of the University. Camp _v. Jeffer, Mangels, Butler & Marmaro
9 ||(1995) 35 Cal.App.4th 620; McKennon v. Nashville Banner Pub. Co. (1995) 115 S.Ct. 879, 886.
10 SIXTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE |
11 6. As a sixth affirmative defense, any California claim for employment
12 ||discrimination and/or retaliation is barred to the extent it relies on allegations that were not
13 || contained in a timely complaint filed with the California Department of Fair Employment and
14 || Housing against each or any Answering Defendant.
15 SEVENTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
16 7. As a seventh affirmative defense, the cause of action for tortious violation of
17 || California public policy fails because Plaintiff has not alleged she was terminated or suspended
18 || fora disciplinary reason. Tameny v. Atlantic Richfield Company (1980) 27 Cal.3d 167.
19 EIGHTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
20 8. As an eighth affirmative defense to the Complaint, the Complaint is barred in
21 || that the exclusive remedy for Plaintiff's claims is the grievance procedure under the employer's
22 |\ grievance policies.
23 NINTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
24 9. As a ninth affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred in that any and all
25 ||conduct by Answering Defendants about which Plaintiff complains was a just and proper
26 \lexercise of management discretion and/or justified by legitimate, non-retaliatory business
27 || purposes or necessity.
28

2 10. Asa tenth affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred in that it is uncertain
3 |Ibecause it does not allege a public policy based on a statute, a California constitutional
4 || provision, or an administrative regulation. Gantt v. Sentry Ins. (1992) 1 Cal.4th 1087, 1090.
5 ELEVENTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
6 11. Asan eleventh affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred in that Answering
7 || Defendants, other than University of San Francisco, were not the employer of Plaintiff.
8 TWELFTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
9 12. As a twelfth affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred by the doctrine of
10 |} laches.
11 THIRTEENTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE |
12 13. Asa thirteenth affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred by the doctrine of
13 || estoppel.
14 FOURTEENTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
15 14. Asa fourteenth affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred by the doctrine of
16 || unclean hands.
17 FIFTEENTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
18 15. Asa fifteenth affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred by the doctrine of
19 || waiver.
20 SIXTEENTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
21 16. Asa sixteenth affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred in that Plaintiff has
22 ||not sustained any injury or damage by reason of any act or omission of any Answering
23 || Defendants and/or any of its or their employee(s) and/or agent(s).
24 SEVENTEENTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
25 17. | Asaseventeenth affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred in that any legally
26 ||recoverable damages suffered by Plaintiff, if any, were not proximately caused by any act or
27. || omission of Answering Defendants.
28

| EIGHTEENTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
2 18. As a eighteenth affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred and Plaintiff is
3 ||precluded from any relief herein because she has failed to mitigate her damages, if any.
4 NINTEENTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
5 19. Asanineteenth affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred in that any claim for
6 || compensatory damages arising from Plaintiff's employment is barred because Plaintiff's sole and
7 {exclusive remedy is governed by the California Workers' Compensation Act, California Labor
8 || Code § 3200, et seg.
9 TWENTIETH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
10 20. As a twentieth affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred in that Plaintiff's
11 ||claims for compensatory damages are barred as to any cause of action for which such relief is
12. ||not available, |
13 TWENTY-FIRST AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
14 21. Asa twenty-first affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred in that Plaintiffs
15 |\claim for attorneys’ fees is barred because such relief is not available for Plaintiff's cause(s) of
16 |] action.
17 TWENTY-SECOND AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
18 22. Asatwenty-second affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred in that Plaintiff's
19 |\claims for punitive damages are barred as to any cause of action for which such relief is not
20 || available.
21 TWENTY-THIRD AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
22 23. As a twenty-third affirmative defense to the Complaint, Answering Defendants
23 = |j/are not liable for punitive damages because Answering Defendants did not (a) authorize or ratify
24 || any wrongful conduct, or (b) have advance knowledge of the unfitness of any employee or
25 ||employ any employee with a conscious disregard of the rights and safety of others. California
26 || Civil Code § 3294(b).
27 TWENTY-FOURTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
28 24. As atwenty-fourth affirmative defense to the Complaint, any claim for punitive

1 ||or exemplary damages is barred in that the California statute on punitive damages is
2 |junconstitutional under the due process clause of the Fourteenth Amendment to the U.S.
3 || Constitution, is unconstitutionally vague and therefore void, is violative of the Equal Protection
4 || Clause, and imposes an undue burden on Interstate Commerce.
5
6 TWENTY-FIFTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
7 25. Asa twenty-fifth affirmative defense, the Complaint is preempted by federal law
3 to the extent Plaintiff seeks to recover employment benefits under employment benefit plans
9 regulated by the Employee Retirement Income Security Act (“ERISA”), 29 U.S.C. § 1001, ef
10 seq. |
i TWENTY-SIXTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
2 26. Asatwenty-sixth affirmative defense to the Complaint, the Complaint is barred
B in that any employment decision about which Plaintiff complains would have been the same in
14 the absence of any alleged discrimination. Price Waterhouse v. Hopkins (1989) 490 U.S. 228.
15 TWENTY-SEVENTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
16 27. As a twenty-seventh affirmative defense, to the extent Plaintiff suffered any
7 symptoms of mental or emotional distress or injury, it was the result of a pre-existing
18 psychological disorder or alternative concurrent cause, and not the result of any act or omission
19 of Answering Defendants.
20 TWENTY-EIGHTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
1 28. Asa twenty-eighth affirmative defense to the Complaint, each of the Answering
9 Defendants other than University of San Francisco assert that the Complaint fails to state a
33 claim for relief against them because it alleges no acts or conduct by them and/or because they
34 were not the employer.
25 COMPLAINT — PRAYER FOR RELIEF
26 With regard to Plaintiff's Prayer for Relief, Answering Defendants deny that such relief
27 |/is either appropriate or justified in the circumstances of this case.
28 WHEREFORE, Answering Defendants pray for judgment as follows:

2 ||any part and/or all of the entire Complaint, and that the Complaint be dismissed in its entirety
3 || and/or in any part with prejudice;
4 2. That Answering Defendants be awarded their costs of suit incurred herein;
5 3. That Answering Defendants be awarded reasonable attorneys’ fees as may be
6 || determined by the Court; and
7 4. For such other and further relief as the Court may deem just and proper.
